summary: Gruber critiques Joanna Stern's Wall Street Journal column about webcam security, arguing that her experiment actually demonstrated how well modern OS security works rather than how vulnerable webcams are. In both the Windows 10 and MacBook tests, the security expert could only access the cameras after the user deliberately disabled multiple security features and ignored explicit warnings. Gruber argues that webcam paranoia is irrational given that post-2008 Macs have hardware-tied indicator LEDs that cannot be disabled by software, citing a former Apple engineer. He finds it inconsistent that people tape over webcams but ignore microphones, which have no indicator lights and can't be blocked by tape. His core argument is that if your system is compromised enough for someone to access your camera, the webcam is the least of your problems.
key_points: Stern's webcam hack required deliberately disabling multiple layers of built-in security protections on both Mac and Windows, undermining the article's alarming framing
Post-2008 Macs have webcam indicator LEDs tied to a hardware signal, making it impossible for software to activate the camera without the light turning on
The mass paranoia over webcams is irrational given the complete lack of similar concern about microphones, which have no indicator lights and can't be physically blocked
Third-party anti-malware software on Mac causes more problems than it solves, since users gullible enough to disable OS security features would likely disable anti-malware too
If malware has compromised your system enough to access the camera, it can also access your keyboard, files, and everything else — the camera is the least of your worries
money_quotes: The path to compromising Stern's cameras was like a test of your home security that starts with a request that you leave your door unlocked and turn off your alarm system.

I have never understood the mass paranoia over laptop webcams — which have in-use indicator lights — and the complete lack of similar paranoia over microphones, which cannot be blocked by a piece of tape and which have no in-use indicator lights.

If someone is willing to ignore the warning from MacOS that an app isn't from a verified developer, and is willing to disable the security settings in that app at the behest of a social engineering hacker, why wouldn't that same person be gullible enough to also disable their anti-malware software?
Malware that can surreptitiously engage your camera can do all sort of other nefarious things. If you can't trust your camera, you can't trust your keyboard either.
The problem isn't your camera, it's malware.
If you open a document that prompts you for access to the camera, aren't you expecting it to be able to access your camera?
And if you do have malware on your Mac, the webcam is likely the least of your problems.
key_insight: Webcam paranoia is security theater that fixates on the most visible threat while ignoring that a compromised system exposes everything — and modern OS protections already work well when users don't deliberately bypass them.
